discriminating_assertions: ci-triggering-mechanism (eval 2): with_skill uses gh-aw-native github-token-for-extra-empty-commit; baseline uses generic GitHub App approach
protected-files-config (eval 2): with_skill knows gh-aw's protected-files: safe output; baseline uses repo rulesets
blocks-pr-approval (eval 2): with_skill recommends allowed-events on submit-pull-request-review; baseline uses branch protection
platform-restore-23769 (eval 3): with_skill knows about the platform auto-restore from gh-aw#23769; baseline doesn't
integrity-filtering (eval 3): with_skill recommends min-integrity: approved; baseline has no mention
non_discriminating_assertions: Eval 1 (slash-command): Both versions scored 6/6 — Sonnet already knows slash_command basics from training data
passive-data-principle: Both versions explain this well — it's fundamental security knowledge
pwn-request-threat-model: Both versions cover this — GitHub Security Lab reference is widely known
analysis.skill_gap: The skill's value is strongest on gh-aw-specific features (protected-files, allowed-events, github-token-for-extra-empty-commit, integrity filtering, platform restore) that aren't in general training data. For well-known patterns (slash_command, pwn-request), the baseline performs equally well.
analysis.missed_by_skill: Eval 3 with_skill did not mention Checkout-GhAwPr.ps1 by name despite it being in references/architecture.md. The skill emphasized platform-native restore over the defense-in-depth script.
